name: Vasubandhu (Abhidharma phase)
dates_notes: This entry isolates the author of the Abhidharma-Kośa-Bhāṣya from the author of the Yogācāra Triṃśikā / Viṃśatikā / Trisvabhāva-Nirdeśa, the second of whom is treated separately under the existing Vasubandhu entry. On Frauwallner's two-Vasubandhus hypothesis (On the Date of the Buddhist Master of the Law Vasubandhu, 1951), an earlier Kośakāra in the late fourth century is distinct from the Yogācāra Vasubandhu of the early fifth. On the single-Vasubandhu reading (Schmithausen, Anacker 1984; Gold 2015 and 2014, Paving the Great Way), one author shifted from Sarvāstivāda Vaibhāṣika exposition to Sautrāntika critique within the same auto-commentary and then to Yogācāra under Asaṅga's influence. The c. 320–400 CE window covers the Abhidharma-stage on either reconstruction; the present entry isolates that doctrinal phase, leaving the question of personal identity flagged.

lineage_polemical.note: Saṅghabhadra's Nyāyānusāra is constructed expressly as a verse-by-verse defence of the orthodox Vaibhāṣika position against the Sautrāntika-leaning glosses Vasubandhu inserts in his auto-commentary on the Kośa-Kārikās. The polemic is intra-Buddhist and intra-Abhidharmic, and the canonical evidence for the Sarvāstivāda-Sautrāntika split as a live debate at the moment Vedāntic critics began to register it.

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–20 mounts the canonical Vedāntic refutation of the Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika position. The sūtras target the foundational sarvam asti thesis and the kṣaṇika-vāda (momentariness) on which it rests; the Kośa-Bhāṣya is the principal systematic exposition of those theses available to the Vedāntic critic.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.18–20 develops the Vedāntic refutation of the Sarvāstivāda position: the kṣaṇika-vāda cannot account for recognition (pratyabhijñā) across moments without smuggling in the very kind of persisting substrate it denies; the doctrine of momentary dharmas with svabhāva therefore collapses under the same considerations Vasubandhu's own Sautrāntika gloss had already begun to press.

Madhva's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya and Anu-Vyākhyāna 2.2.18–20 extend the Sarvāstivāda refutation in a realist direction: the Vaibhāṣika atomism and momentariness fail because dependent reality requires both eternal independent (svatantra) Brahman and eternally distinct dependent (paratantra) substances, neither of which the Sarvāstivāda ontology can deliver.

core_thesis: Vasubandhu's Abhidharma-Kośa-Bhāṣya is the major systematic exposition of Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma in classical Indian philosophy. The verses (kārikā) state the orthodox Vaibhāṣika doctrine; the auto-commentary (bhāṣya) often reformulates it from a Sautrāntika standpoint that recognises only the present moment as actually existent. The foundational Sarvāstivāda thesis is sarvam asti — past, present, and future dharmas all exist as such, in their respective temporal modes (adhva). This is not the trivial claim that everything exists; it is the technical claim that a dharma is a real (dravyasat), momentary (kṣaṇika) constituent of experience whose identity persists across the temporal modes even though its causal efficacy is restricted to the present. The Kośa develops this through the standard Abhidharma topics: the seventy-five dharmas (dhātu, āyatana, skandha analysis), the four conditioned (saṃskṛta) characteristics (jāti, sthiti, jarā, anityatā), the doctrine of pratītya-samutpāda as twelve-link conditioning (nidāna), and the karman-and-vipāka analysis. The bhāṣya repeatedly stages the Sautrāntika critique against this orthodoxy: the future and past cannot be existent in the same way the present is, on pain of either collapsing the present's distinctive causal efficacy or turning past and future into present. The Sautrāntika alternative — only the present moment exists, past and future being conceptual constructions on the seeds (bīja) deposited in the cognitive series — anticipates the Yogācāra ālaya-vijñāna as the carrier of those seeds and supplies the philosophical motive for the developed Vasubandhu Triṃśikā. The text is therefore both the canonical Abhidharma compendium and the document of its own internal critique — the standard reference both for the orthodox *sarvāstivāda* position and for the *sautrāntika* dissent that grew within it.

engaged_works.title: Abhidharma-Kosha-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Vasubandhu in the Sanskrit verse-form preserved in Tibetan and Chinese transmissions and partially in Sanskrit (Pradhan 1967 reconstruction).

ascription_evidence.description: Pradhan ed. 1967; La Vallée Poussin trans. 1923–1931; Cox 1995, Disputed Dharmas, on the verse-bhāṣya relation.

engaged_works.summary: The Kośa-Kārikā in eight chapters (later expanded by a ninth on the refutation of the substantial self) supplies the verse-form summary of orthodox Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika Abhidharma. The chapters cover dhātu (the eighteen elements), indriya (the twenty-two faculties), loka (the world-system and rebirth-paths), karman (the analysis of action), anuśaya (the latent defilements), pudgala-mārga (the path-stages), jñāna (the ten knowledges), and samāpatti (the meditative attainments). The verses present the Mahā-Vibhāṣā doctrine in its most compressed and learnable form and have remained the standard introduction to Abhidharma in Tibetan and East Asian Buddhist scholasticism for fifteen centuries. The internal-critique apparatus appears not in the verses but in the auto-commentary; the verses themselves are the orthodox exposition the bhāṣya then problematises, and they preserve the canonical formulation of sarvam asti and of the conditioned-dharma analysis on which the school's later doctrinal history is built.

engaged_works.title: Abhidharma-Kosha-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The auto-commentary is securely Vasubandhu's. The Sautrāntika-leaning glosses are the principal philosophical content of the work and the proximate cause of Saṅghabhadra's Nyāyānusāra.

ascription_evidence.description: Pradhan ed. 1967; Gold 2015, Paving the Great Way; Cox 1995.

engaged_works.summary: Vasubandhu's prose auto-commentary on his own Kośa-Kārikās, structured as a verse-by-verse exegesis but functioning philosophically as the major internal critique of Sarvāstivāda orthodoxy from a Sautrāntika standpoint. The bhāṣya repeatedly stages a pūrvapakṣa in the orthodox Vaibhāṣika voice (the verses' apparent doctrine) and then advances objections from a Sautrāntika position that recognises only the present moment as dravyasat. The decisive site of this critique is the treatment of sarvam asti at AKBh 5.25–27, where Vasubandhu canvasses the four classical Sarvāstivāda accounts of how a dharma can be said to exist in past, present, and future modes (Dharmatrāta's bhāva-anyathātva, Ghoṣaka's lakṣaṇa-anyathātva, Vasumitra's avasthā-anyathātva, Buddhadeva's anyathā-anyathātva), endorses Vasumitra's view as least objectionable for the orthodoxy, and then advances the Sautrāntika alternative according to which only the present is actual and past-and-future are conceptual constructions on the seed-series. The bhāṣya is therefore both the principal Sanskrit exposition of Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma and the document of the Sautrāntika critique that motivated the move to Yogācāra. The ninth chapter, Pudgala-Pratiṣedha, contains the full anātman argument against the Vātsīputrīya pudgala-vāda.

reception_notes: The Kośa-Bhāṣya is the principal textual basis for the Vedāntic refutation of Sarvāstivāda at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–20. Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya on those sūtras engages the Sarvāstivāda position systematically, addressing the kṣaṇika-vāda, the sarvam asti thesis, and the failure of the momentary-dharma ontology to account for recognition (pratyabhijñā) across moments. Madhva's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya and Anu-Vyākhyāna extend this engagement in a realist direction. The Kośa-Bhāṣya is also the document on which Saṅghabhadra's Nyāyānusāra is constructed as polemical defence, making it the pivot of the Sarvāstivāda-Sautrāntika debate as it stood at the moment of the Vedāntic critique.
